---
title: Week 03 -- `Tidyverse` Single-Table Queries 
subtitle: AAI1001 -- Data Engineering and Visualization 
author: Guang Hua
date: 2026-01-19
date-format: "dddd MMM DD, YYYY"

toc: true
toc-depth: 2
number-sections: true
number-depth: 2
engine: knitr
format:
  revealjs:
    self-contained: true
    embed-resources: true
    theme: [default, custom.scss]
    width: 1600
    height: 900
    margin: 0.05
    min-scale: 0.2
    max-scale: 2.0
    scrollable: true
    slide-number: true
    preview-links: false
    incremental: true
    logo: imgs/sit_logo_mark.png
    footer:  AAI1001 -- AY25/26 Tri 2 (Assisted by GenAI)
    html-table-processing: none
    code-block-height: 700px
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(knitr)
library(gt)
library(tidyverse)
```


## Updated Weekly Schedule {.unnumbered .unlisted}

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

timetable <- tribble(
  ~Instructor, ~Week, ~`Monday (9–11am)`, ~`Thursday (9–11am)`,
  "Prof. Hua", "01", "Intro to `RStudio` and `Quarto`", "Software Setup: `RStudio`, `Quarto`, and `Git`",
  "Prof. Hua", "02", "Intro to `R`", "**Assessment 1** (*Individual 1*)",
  "Prof. Hua", "03", "`tidyverse` Single-Table Queries", "Team Project Description, Q&A",
  "Prof. Hua", "04", "`tidyverse` Join-Table Queries", "**Assessment 2** (*Teamwork 1*)",
  "Prof. Hua", "05", "Time Series Basics", "Team Project Q&A",
  "Prof. Hua", "06", "Data Engineering Case Study", "**Assessment 3** (*Individual 2*)",
  "Prof. Hua", "08", "Data Visualization Using `ggplot2`", "Intro to `Typst`, Team Project Q&A",
  "Prof. Cautis", "09", "Exploring `geom`", "**Assessment 4** (*Individual 3*), Project Proposal Deadline",
  "Prof. Cautis", "10", "Aesthetic Mappings", "Data Visualization Case Study, Team Project Q&A",
  "Prof. Cautis", "11", "Intro to `gt` Tables", "**Assessment 5** (*Teamwork 2*)",
  "Prof. Cautis", "12", "Visualizing Geospatial Data", "Team Project Q&A",
  "All", "13", "Team Project Evaluation (TBA)", "Team Project Evaluation (TBA)",
) |>
  gt(
    groupname_col = "Instructor",
    row_group_as_column = TRUE
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = 28,
    column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    table.align = "left",
    table.width = pct(100),
    table.font.names = "source sans pro",
    data_row.padding = px(3)
  ) |>
  cols_align(
    align = "center",
    columns = "Week"
  ) |>
  fmt_markdown(columns = c(1,2,3,4)) |>
  tab_style(
    cell_text(align = "center", v_align = "middle", weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_row_groups()
  ) |>
  cols_width(
    Instructor ~ px(200),
    `Week` ~ px(160),
    `Monday (9–11am)` ~ px(540),
    `Thursday (9–11am)` ~ px(700)
  )

timetable |>
tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "lightyellow"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = 3)
  ) 
```


## Introduction

```{css, echo=FALSE}
/* for code chunk */
code.sourceCode {
  font-size: 1.3em;
}

/* for R output */
.reveal .cell-output-stdout {
  font-size: 1.3em; 
}
```

<hr>

Previously, we have covered the basic and essential skills of `R`. In this lesson, you will be guided through the process of using `tidyverse` to query a single table stored in a data frame in `R`. We will use `readr`, `tibble`, `dplyr`, and `tidyr` packages. With them, you can extract valuable insights and answer questions that drive informed decision-making. For instance:

1.  What was the total number of passengers on the `Titanic`?
2.  How many individuals belonged to each passenger class?
3.  What is the difference in survival rates between women and men?
4.  What was the mean age of the surviving crew members?

. . .

::: {style="font-weight: bold; color: red; font-size: 0.8em;"}
Create a `week_03_tidyverse_single_table.qmd` file in `RStudio` and follow along with the lesson.
:::

``` md
---
title: "Week 03 `tidyverse` Single-Table Query"
author: Your Name

format:
  html:
    embed-resources: true
---
```


## Importing the `Titanic` Data {style="font-size: 0.95em;"}

<hr>

![](imgs/readr.png){.absolute bottom="50" right="36" width="150"}

The raw data for the `Titanic` used in this lesson are 3 `.csv` files. The first step is to import the data into `R` using the `read_csv()` function from the `readr` package. To do so, we can load either `tidyverse` or `readr` into the current session.

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

# Load the tidyverse package
library(tidyverse)

# or load the readr package
library(readr)
```

. . .

Next, we import the data (read `read_csv()` [documentation](https://readr.tidyverse.org/reference/read_delim.html){target="_blank"} for more details):

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true

# Import the data from the `.csv` files
people <- read_csv("data/titanic/people.csv", show_col_types=FALSE)
tickets <- read_csv("data/titanic/tickets.csv", show_col_types=FALSE)
lifeboats <- read_csv("data/titanic/lifeboats.csv", show_col_types=FALSE)
```

If the code is successfully executed, then the 3 data frames will appear under **Environment** tab.

. . .

::: {.callout-note style="width: 80%;"}
Note that it is possible to import the `.csv` files into a `SQL` database and query the data using `SQL` commands. In practice, which method to choose depends on the size of the data, complexity of queries, specific task requirements, and other considerations.
:::




## A Glance at `tibble`

<hr>

![](imgs/tibble.png){.absolute bottom="50" right="36" width="150"}

The `tibble` is a modern data frame that is part of the `tidyverse`. It is a more user-friendly version of the traditional `data.frame` in `R`. It is easier to read, print, and manipulate. Let's take a look at the `people` data frame:

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true

class(people)
```

. . .

An example of `R`'s built-in `data.frame`:
```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true

class(mtcars)
mtcars_df <- mtcars
```

. . .

Casting `data.frame` as `tibble`:

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true

mtcar_tbl <- as_tibble(mtcars)
```

Have a look at `mtcars_df` and `mtcars_tbl`, what do you observe?

. . . 

::: {.callout-note style="width: 60%;"}
More information about `tibble` can be found [here](https://r4ds.had.co.nz/tibbles.html){target="_blank"}.
:::


## A Glance at `dplyr` {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

<hr>

`dplyr` is a grammar of data manipulation, providing a consistent set of verbs that help you solve the most common data manipulation challenges. `dplyr` functions for single-table query include:

- Row-Wise:
    + `filter()` chooses rows based on column values.
    + `distinct()` chooses unique rows.
    + `count()` counts rows.
    + `slice()` chooses rows based on location.
    + `arrange()` changes the order of the rows.

- Column-Wise:
    + `select()` changes whether or not a column is included.
    + `rename()` changes the name of columns.
    + `mutate()` changes the values of columns and creates new columns.

- Groups-Wise:
    + `summarise()` collapses a group into a single row.


::: {.callout-note style="width: 60%;"}
More information about `dplyr` can be found [here](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/){target="_blank"}.
:::

![](imgs/dplyr.png){.absolute bottom="50" right="36" width="150"}



## Selecting Columns {.smaller}

<hr>

`select()` with `tidyselect` functions as arguments.
    
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

people |>
  select(fam_name, given_name) |>
  head(3)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

people |>
  select(ends_with("name"))|>
  head(3)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

people |>
  select(contains("_")) |>
  head(3)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

people |>
  select(age:ticket) |>
  head(3)
```

::: {.callout-note}
More information about `select()` can be found [here](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/select.html){target="_blank"}.
:::


## Counting Rows and Columns {.smaller}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

ncol(people)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

nrow(people)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

count(people)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

count(
  people,
  missing = if_else(
    is.na(age),
    "yes",
    "no"
  )
)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

count(
  people,
  missing = if_else(
    is.na(age),
    "yes",
    "no"
  ),
  gender = sex,
  name = "num"
)
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

distinct(people, embarkation_port)
```


```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

count(
  people,
  embarkation_port,
  name = "num"
)
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

count(people, embarkation_port, sex)
```
:::
::::

::: {.callout-note}
More information about `count()` can be found [here](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/count.html){target="_blank"}.
:::


## Filtering and Sorting Rows (1) {style="font-size: 0.6em;"}

<hr>


:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   `filter()` mirrors `WHERE` clause in `SQL`

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment


filter(people, (age <= 20 & class == "3rd")) |>
  select(fam_name, given_name, age) |>
  head(4)
    ```

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

sum(is.na(pull(people, age) <= 20))
    ```

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

people |>
  filter(age <= 20) |>
  pull(age) |>
  anyNA()
    ```

-   Create `engineer_bodies` data frame (similar to `VIEW` in `SQL`)

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment


engineer_bodies <-
  people |>
  filter(
    str_detect(profession, "[Ee]ngineer") |
      str_detect(job_on_board, "[Ee]ngineer"),
    !is.na(recovered_body)
  ) |>
  mutate(ticket_num = as.integer(ticket)) |>
  select(fam_name, given_name, age, ticket_num, recovered_body)
engineer_bodies |> head(4)
    ```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
-   `arrange()` mirrors `ORDER BY` clause in `SQL` (default is ascending)

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment

arrange(engineer_bodies, age) |> head(7) # desc(age)
    ```

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

engineer_bodies |>
  distinct(ticket_num) |>
  mutate(ticket_num_is_na = is.na(ticket_num)) |>
  arrange(desc(ticket_num_is_na), ticket_num, ) |>
  head(5)
    ```

    <br>

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment

arrange(engineer_bodies, desc(age), recovered_body) |> head(6)
    ```

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false
#| output-location: fragment

# Test these functions
slice_min(engineer_bodies, order_by = age, n = 5)
slice_max(engineer_bodies, order_by = age, n = 5)
    ```
:::
::::

## 7 Filtering and Sorting Rows (2) {.unnumbered style="font-size: 0.9em;"}

<hr>

-   Ranking: `ROW_NUMBER()`, `RANK()`, and `DENSE_RANK()` correspond to `row_number()`, `min_rank()`, and `dense_rank()`

    ```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment


    engineer_bodies |>
      select(fam_name, given_name, age) |>
      arrange(desc(age)) |>
      mutate(
        row_number = row_number(desc(age)),
        min_rank = min_rank(desc(age)),
        dense_rank = dense_rank(desc(age))
        ) |>
      head(5)
    ```

::: {.callout-note}
`mutate()` creates new columns that are functions of existing variables. More information about `mutate()` can be found [here](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/mutate.html){target="_blank"}.
:::

## Summarizing Data by Group

<hr>

For each class on `Titanic`, how many male and female passengers survived, and what are the mean and standard deviation values of their ages?

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

# Cannot control execution output number precision
people |>
  summarize(
    n_people = n(),
    mean_age = round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2), # set 2 decimals but not displayed
    sd_age = sd(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = c(sex, class, survived)
  ) |>
  filter(class != "Crew", survived == TRUE) |>
  arrange(sex, class)
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: false

people |>
  mutate(
    mean_age = round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2), # set 2 decimals but not displayed
    sd_age = sd(age, na.rm = TRUE)
  ) |>
  count(sex, class, survived, name = "n_people")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
More information about `dplyr::summarize()` can be found [here](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/summarise.html){target="_blank"}.
:::



## Pivoting: `pivot_wider()` {style="font-size: 0.7em;"}

<hr>

![](imgs/tidyr.png){.absolute bottom="50" right="36" width="120"}

-   Pivoting is the process of transforming a table from a long format (more rows than columns) to a wide format (more columns than rows) or vice versa. 
-   `tidyr::pivot_longer()` and `tidyr::pivot_wider()` are used to pivot data frames.
-   Example---count jobs on board Titanic per embarkation port `pivot_wider()`:

. . .

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment


job_per_port <-
  people |>
  filter(!is.na(job_on_board)) |>
  count(embarkation_port, job_on_board, name = "num")
job_per_port
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment


pivot_wider(
  job_per_port,
  names_from = embarkation_port,
  values_from = num,
  values_fill = 0
)
```
:::
::::




## Pivoting: `pivot_longer()` {style="font-size: 0.65em;"}

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: nonincremental
-   Example---age statistics per class on Titanic `pivot_longer()`: 
:::

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: fragment


age_stats <-
  people |>
  summarize(
    min = min(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    lower_quartile = quantile(age, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE),
    median = median(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    upper_quartile = quantile(age, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE),
    max = max(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = class
  )
age_stats
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: true
#| output-location: column-fragment

pivot_longer(
  age_stats,
  cols = c(
    min,
    lower_quartile,
    median,
    upper_quartile,
    max
  ),
  names_to = "stat",
  values_to = "value"
) |>
  arrange(class, value) |>
  gt()
```
:::
::::

![](imgs/tidyr.png){.absolute bottom="50" left="36" width="120"}



## In-Class Exercise 1: Answer Questions at Beginning of Lesson {style="font-size: 0.85em;"}

<hr>

::::columns 
::: {.column width="50%"}

1.  What was the total number of passengers on the `Titanic`? Generate a single element table with column name `num`.

    ```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

people |>
  count(name = "num")
    ```

    ---

2.  How many individuals belonged to each passenger class? Generate a table with columns `class` and `num`. <br> Hint: use `count()` or `summarize()`.

    ```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

people |>
  count(class, name = "num")

people |>
  summarize(
    num = n(), 
    .by = class
  ) |>
  arrange(class)
    ```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}

3.  What is the difference in survival rates between women and men? Generate a table with columns `sex` and `rate`.

    ```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

summarize(
  people,
  rate = round(mean(survived, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
  .by = sex
)
    ```

    ---

4.  What was the mean age of the surviving crew members? Generate a single element table with column name `mean age`. <br> Hint: `filter()` then `summarize()`, or `summarize()` then `filter()`.

    ```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

people |>
  filter(class == "Crew", survived == TRUE) |>
  summarize(
    "mean age" = round(mean(age, na.rm = TRUE), 2)
  )


people |>
  summarize(
    "mean age" = mean(age, na.rm = TRUE),
    .by = class
  ) |>
  filter(class == "Crew") |>
  select("mean age")
    ```


:::
::::

## In-Class Exercise 2: A Toy Model {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

<hr>

Suppose we propose the following `death_index`: [$\texttt{death_index} = \texttt{age} + 10 \times \texttt{numeric_class} + 30 \times \texttt{numeric_sex}$]{style="border: 1px solid; margin: 30px;"}

Encoding: `numeric_class`: `1st` $\rightarrow$ 1, `2nd` $\rightarrow$ 2, `3rd` $\rightarrow$ 3, `Crew` $\rightarrow$ 4. `numeric_sex`: `Female` $\rightarrow$ 0,  `Male` $\rightarrow$ 1.

<hr>

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}

Task 1: Create a table with columns `fam_name`, `given_name`, `age`, `numeric_class`, `numeric_sex`, `survived`, and `death_index`.


```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

tb <- people |>
  mutate(
    numeric_class = case_when(
      class == "1st" ~ 1,
      class == "2nd" ~ 2,
      class == "3rd" ~ 3,
      class == "Crew" ~ 4
      ),
    numeric_sex = case_when(
      sex == "Female" ~ 0,
      sex == "Male" ~ 1
      )
    ) |>
  select(fam_name, given_name, age, numeric_class, numeric_sex, survived) |> 
  mutate(death_index = age + 10 * numeric_class + 30 * numeric_sex) 

tb |>
  head(10) |>
  gt()
```
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
Task 2: Create a table with columns `numeric_class`, `numeric_sex`, `survive_rate`, and `mean_death_index`.

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

tb |>
  summarize(
    survive_rate = round(mean(survived, na.rm = TRUE), 4),
    mean_death_index = round(mean(death_index, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    .by = c(numeric_class, numeric_sex)
    ) |>
  arrange(desc(survive_rate), desc(mean_death_index)) |>
  gt()
```

What do you observe from the result? Is `death_index` a good predictor of survival rate?

:::
::::

## In-Class Exercise 3: Group Passengers on `Titanic` {style="font-size: 0.8em;"}

<hr>

`Titanic` passengers with a shared ticket can be considered a ticket group. Typically, a ticket group comprised family members, friends, or domestic workers employed by the family.

**Question: How many ticket groups lost at least one member at sea?** Hint: check `group_by()` info [here](https://dplyr.tidyverse.org/reference/group_by.html){target="_blank"}. 

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: false

people |>
  filter(!is.na(ticket)) |>
  group_by(ticket) |>
  summarize(
    n_people = n(),
    n_survived = sum(survived)
  ) |>
  filter(n_people > 1 & n_people > n_survived) |>
  count()
```



## Appendix 1 --- Content of `Titanic` Database {.unnumbered}

```{r}
#| echo: false

titanic_tables <-
  tribble(
    ~Table_Name, ~Column_Name, ~Description,
    "people", "id",
    "Unique integer-valued identifier for each person",
    "people", "fam_name",
    "Family name",
    "people", "given_name",
    "Given name",
    "people", "age",
    "Age in years",
    "people", "class",
    "`Crew` or class of travel (`1st`, `2nd`, or `3rd`)",
    "people", "gender",
    "`Male` or `Female`",
    "people", "survived",
    str_c(
      "Either `TRUE` or `FALSE`, indicating whether the person survived the ",
      "sinking"
    ),
    "people", "ticket",
    "Character string identifying the ticket",
    "people", "embarkation_port",
    "Port where the person first boarded the Titanic",
    "people", "profession",
    "Profession of the person",
    "people", "job_on_board",
    "Job on board (e.g., `Engineering Crew`, `Servant`, or `Musician`)",
    "people", "lifeboat",
    "Lifeboat number or letter",
    "people", "certain_on_lifeboat",
    str_c(
      "Either `TRUE` or `FALSE`, indicating strength of evidence that ",
      "the person was on the lifeboat identified in the `lifeboat` column"
    ),
    "people", "recovered_body",
    "Numeric identifier of the dead body recovered after the sinking",
    "tickets", "ticket",
    str_c(
      "Character string identifying the ticket. Please note that the ticket ",
      "identifiers in the data source (Hind 2023) are not unique. In the ",
      "present data, the family name was added to the original ticket ",
      "identifier unless there was convincing evidence that the person ",
      "truly shared their ticket with another person."
    ),
    "tickets", "price",
    "Fare paid in British pounds, shillings, and pence",
    "lifeboats", "lifeboat",
    "Lifeboat number or letter",
    "lifeboats", "boat_name",
    "Name of the lifeboat in the Encyclopedia Titanica",
    "lifeboats", "boat_info",
    str_c(
      "Additional text information about the lifeboat provided by the ",
      "Encyclopedia Titanica"
    )
  )
gt(titanic_tables, groupname_col = "Table_Name") |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_text(font = system_fonts(name = "monospace-code")),
    locations = list(
      cells_row_groups(),
      cells_body(columns = Column_Name)
    )
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    Column_Name = "Column Name",
  ) |>
  fmt_markdown(columns = "Description") |>
  tab_options(
    table.font.size = 20,
    data_row.padding = px(3),
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    row_group.background.color = "grey50",
    row_group.font.weight = "bold"
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "grey95"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = seq(1, nrow(titanic_tables), by = 2))
  )
```





## Appendix 2 --- `tidyverse` vs. `PostgreSQL` {.unnumbered}

<hr>

```{r}
#| echo: false

tribble(
  ~`tidyverse`, ~`PostgreSQL`,
  "`select()`",
  "`SELECT`",
  "`count()`",
  "`COUNT`",
  "`filter()`",
  "`WHERE`",
  "`distinct()`",
  "`DISTINCT`",
  "`arrange()`",
  "`ORDER BY`",
  "`desc()`",
  "`DESC`",
  "`slice_min()`",
  "`ORDER BY ...`<br>`LIMIT ...`",
  "`slice_max()`",
  "`ORDER BY ... DESC`<br>`LIMIT ...`",
  "`row_number()`",
  "`ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY ...)`",
  "`min_rank()`",
  "`RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ...)`",
  "`dense_rank()`",
  "`DENSE_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ...)`",
  "`summarize()`",
  "`SELECT aggregate_function ...`<br>`GROUP BY ...`",
  "`pivot_wider()`",
  "`CROSSTAB()`",
  "`pivot_longer()`",
  "Not built into `PostgreSQL`. See [Stack Overflow](https://tinyurl.com/3zk7cnvm)."
) |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_markdown() |>
  cols_label(
    tidyverse = md("`Tidyverse` Function"),
    PostgreSQL = md("`PostgreSQL` Equivalent")
  ) |>
  tab_options(
    column_labels.font.weight = "bold",
    column_labels.background.color = "lightblue",
    table.font.size = 26,
    table.align = "left",
    data_row.padding = px(3)
  ) |>
  cols_width(
    tidyverse ~ px(300),
    PostgreSQL ~ px(600)
  ) |>
  tab_style(
    style = cell_fill(color = "gray90"),
    locations = cells_body(rows = seq(2, 14, by = 2))
  )
```
